name: Bazel
summary: Google's open-sourced build system for large monorepos: BUILD files declare per-target visibility (visibility = [...]), enforced by the build graph at build time rather than by the language; WORKSPACE / MODULE.bazel defines the build universe; bzlmod (since 2023) is the modern dependency-management interface.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Bazel** (Google's open-sourced build system) implements per-target visibility declarations: a `BUILD` file lists targets (libraries, binaries, tests) and each target has a `visibility = [...]` attribute restricting which other targets can depend on it. This is enforced by the build graph at build time, not by the language.

Bazel is structurally similar to Buck (`data/buck`) — both descend from Google's internal build system Blaze and Meta's internal version — but they have evolved separately under different organisations. This record covers Bazel; Buck is treated separately.

sections.title: Per-target visibility — build-graph enforcement
sections.content:
This is structurally similar to JPMS's `exports ... to` qualified exports but at the build-graph level rather than the language level. The consequence is that **a single source file can have one set of language-level visibility and a different set of build-system-level visibility**, with the latter being the operative constraint in monorepo deployments.

The interaction with language-level modules is subtle. In a Bazel monorepo using Java, a class can be `public` (Java visibility) but only depended on by specific Bazel targets (Bazel visibility). The build graph enforces architectural boundaries that Java's package-level visibility cannot. Google internally relies on this heavily.

sections.title: WORKSPACE, MODULE.bazel, and bzlmod
sections.content:
Bazel has the most ambitious "workspace" concept among the systems surveyed: `WORKSPACE` (or `MODULE.bazel` in bzlmod) defines an entire build universe with arbitrary internal package boundaries, source dependencies, and remote build artifacts.

**bzlmod** (since 2023) is the modern dependency-management interface, replacing the older `WORKSPACE`-only model. A `MODULE.bazel` file declares dependencies on other Bazel modules in the **Bazel Central Registry** (BCR) plus source-level overrides. The transition has been gradual; large existing Bazel codebases continue to use WORKSPACE for years before migrating.

sections.title: Design lesson — build-system visibility at scale
sections.content:
The lesson is that **at sufficient scale, build-system visibility becomes the dominant abstraction**, not language-level visibility. Languages designed for monorepo use should consider whether their module-system primitives compose cleanly with build-system visibility, or whether the two will fight. Most languages punt on this; Bazel's reach is substantial enough that punting is increasingly costly.

Production: Google internally (the original use case), Twitter/X, Stripe, Pinterest, Uber, and many other large monorepo organisations. The cost is a steep learning curve and significant configuration surface; the benefit is that architectural boundaries become enforceable at the build layer regardless of language.
